my_sentence: I have a good day today.
correction: I had a good day today.
explanation: When you talk about a day that is essentially over (your class is at night), use the past simple. 'I have a good day' describes a present/general state; the completed day takes 'had'.
correct_examples: I had a good day today.
I had a busy day at work yesterday.
context: Small talk at the start of class.

my_sentence: Today was the holiday for Japanese members, so I can do some studying for myself.
correction: Today was a holiday for the Japanese members, so I was able to do some studying for myself.
explanation: For an ability that was realized on a specific past occasion, use 'was/were able to', not the present 'can'. 'Can' here clashes with the past-tense clause before it.
correct_examples: The office was closed, so I was able to finish my own project.
It was a holiday, so I was able to rest.
context: Explaining a holiday gave him free time to study.

my_sentence: Talked something with my coworker.
correction: Talked about something with my coworkers.
explanation: 'Talk' takes 'about' before the topic: 'talk about something'. Also, since there was more than one colleague, use the plural 'coworkers'.
correct_examples: I talked about the project with my coworkers.
We talked about the news over lunch.
context: Describing his day at work.

my_sentence: I eat my dinner. I eat something.
correction: I ate my dinner. / I ate some.
explanation: Dinner is already finished, so use the past simple 'ate'. You repeatedly slipped between 'eat' and 'ate' — anchor completed actions in the past tense.
correct_examples: I ate dinner an hour ago.
I ate some vegetables and fruit.
context: Saying what he had for dinner.

my_sentence: Their families will go bankrupt at the end.
correction: Their families will go bankrupt in the end.
explanation: 'In the end' means 'finally / as the final outcome'. 'At the end' needs an object ('at the end of the month') and refers to a point in time/space, not a final result.
correct_examples: In the end, they lost everything.
At the end of the movie, the hero wins.
context: Describing how gambling ruins families.

my_sentence: One day he addicted to gambling.
correction: One day he got addicted to gambling.
explanation: 'Addicted' is a past participle/adjective, not a verb on its own. Use 'get/become addicted to' for the change, or 'be addicted to' for the state. You made this slip several times today.
correct_examples: He got addicted to gambling.
She is addicted to her phone.
context: Telling the story of a coworker's husband.

my_sentence: He ran out his money.
correction: He ran out of his money.
explanation: The phrasal verb is 'run out of (something)'. The 'of' is required before the object.
correct_examples: He ran out of money.
We ran out of time before finishing.
context: The husband used up all his money gambling.

my_sentence: My coworker had to divorced with him.
correction: My coworker had to divorce him.
explanation: After 'had to' use the base verb ('divorce'), not the past form. Also 'divorce' is transitive — you divorce someone, with no 'with' (assistant note: same goes for 'marry someone').
correct_examples: She had to divorce him.
They divorced last year.
context: The wife divorced her gambling husband.

my_sentence: Her husband, he need to return by himself.
correction: Her husband needs to return it by himself.
explanation: Third-person singular subjects take '-s': 'he needs', not 'he need'. This was a recurring error throughout today's class.
correct_examples: He needs to repay the money himself.
She needs to leave early today.
context: Who is responsible for repaying the debt.

my_sentence: If you kill someone, you will be killed by the government.
correction: If you kill someone, you will be punished by the law.
explanation: Say 'punished by the law' for legal consequences. 'Killed by the government' is too literal and unidiomatic; the law (not the government as an actor) punishes crimes.
correct_examples: Anyone who breaks the law will be punished by the law.
He was punished by the law for fraud.
context: Discussing the death penalty.

my_sentence: There are many companies will harass him every day.
correction: There are many companies that will harass him every day.
explanation: A subject relative clause needs a relative pronoun ('that'/'which'/'who') to link it to the noun. Without 'that', you have two verbs ('are', 'will harass') with no connector.
correct_examples: There are many companies that lend money.
I know a person who can help you.
context: Debt-collection companies harassing the debtor.

my_sentence: If someone don't return your money, you can report to the police.
correction: If someone doesn't return your money, you can report it to the police.
explanation: 'Someone' is third-person singular, so use 'doesn't', not 'don't'. (Also add the object 'it' after 'report'.)
correct_examples: If someone doesn't pay, you can take legal action.
If someone doesn't reply, send a reminder.
context: What to do when a debtor won't repay.

my_sentence: Even if you win the case, you couldn't get your money anymore.
correction: Even if you win the case, you would not be able to get your money back.
explanation: In a hypothetical outcome use 'would not be able to' rather than 'couldn't ... anymore'. 'Be able to' also reads more naturally than bare 'could' for future possibility, and 'get ... back' is the natural phrasing for recovering money.
correct_examples: Even if you win, you would not be able to recover the money.
You would not be able to get it back.
context: Winning a lawsuit but the debtor has nothing.

my_sentence: I don't think we need to depend on one or two person to protect us.
correction: I don't think we need to depend on one or two people to protect us.
explanation: After a number greater than one, use the plural 'people' (the plural of 'person').
correct_examples: We can't depend on one or two people.
Two people were waiting outside.
context: Arguing that society should rely on law, not individuals.

my_sentence: People will change time by time.
correction: People change from time to time.
explanation: The fixed idiom is 'from time to time' (= occasionally). 'Time by time' is not an English expression.
correct_examples: People change from time to time.
From time to time, he visits his hometown.
context: Arguing that leaders can change over time.

my_sentence: Even though the law is not perfect, however, at least it can protect most ordinary people.
correction: Even though the law is not perfect, it can at least protect most ordinary people.
explanation: (assistant note) Don't pair 'even though' with 'however' — they both signal contrast, so using both is redundant. Use one: 'Even though X, Y' OR 'X; however, Y'. You combine them often.
correct_examples: Even though the law is not perfect, it still protects ordinary people.
The law is not perfect; however, it protects ordinary people.
context: Defending the rule of law.

my_sentence: The major need to report for the President.
correction: The mayor needs to report to the President.
explanation: You 'report to' a person or authority, not 'report for'. (Also third-person 'needs', and the word is 'mayor' for a city leader.)
correct_examples: The manager reports to the director.
Each governor reports to the central government.
context: Explaining the chain of command in government.

my_sentence: It started when 1950.
correction: It started in 1950.
explanation: Use 'in' before a year. 'When' introduces a clause (when + subject + verb), not a bare year.
correct_examples: The company started in 1950.
It began in 2008, when the crisis hit.
context: Describing when the government system began.

my_sentence: It started in 1950 when the country built.
correction: It started in 1950, when the country was built.
explanation: The country doesn't build itself — it 'was built', so use the passive 'was/got built'. Without 'was', the sentence reads as if the country actively built something.
correct_examples: The system began when the country was built.
The bridge was built in 1950.
context: The founding of the country.

my_sentence: It did not affect nowadays.
correction: It did not affect us nowadays.
explanation: 'Affect' is transitive and needs an object. Add 'us' (or 'our lives') — you can't 'affect' with nothing being affected.
correct_examples: It did not affect us at the time.
The change didn't affect our daily lives.
context: Saying current policies won't affect people until the future.

my_sentence: It will affect for the future.
correction: It will affect the future.
explanation: 'Affect' takes a direct object with no preposition. Don't insert 'for' (or 'to') after it.
correct_examples: It will affect the future.
This decision will affect our children.
context: Long-term effects of current leadership.

my_sentence: Our current life originated from the leaders of ten years ago.
correction: Our current lives originated from the leaders of ten years ago.
explanation: When 'we/our' refers to many people, use the plural 'lives' (pronounced /laɪvz/), not 'life'. You repeated 'our life nowadays' several times.
correct_examples: Our lives have changed a lot.
Our current lives depend on past decisions.
context: Today's prosperity comes from past leaders.

my_sentence: If you have your own company, you can't affair government.
correction: If you have your own company, you can't oversee the affairs of the government.
explanation: 'Affair' is a noun, not a verb. To manage/be in charge of something, use a verb like 'oversee' or 'handle': 'oversee government affairs'.
correct_examples: Civil servants oversee government affairs.
She handles the affairs of the company.
context: Separation between business and government roles.

my_sentence: You can go any countries.
correction: You can go to any country.
explanation: 'Go' needs 'to' before a destination, and 'any' here takes the singular 'country' ('any country' = whichever one).
correct_examples: With this passport you can go to any country.
You can travel to any country you like.
context: What a diplomatic passport allows.

my_sentence: A person who marry with a Chinese citizen can get a green card.
correction: A person who marries a Chinese citizen can get a green card.
explanation: 'Marry' is transitive — you marry someone, with no 'with'. Also, the relative pronoun 'who' refers to a singular person, so use 'marries'.
correct_examples: A person who marries a citizen can apply for residency.
She married a doctor last year.
context: Getting a green card through marriage.
